EditText.Text:
Adam & Eve Man and Woman
Adam: Of the Earth / grounded Eve: Mother of the living / Life giver
After Adam and Eve eats the fruit of
The tree of knowing good and evil
Banished from paradise To mortality
Then they have children
Unspecified relation I.e. irrelevant / besides the point
“You are free to eat from any tree in the garden; but you must not eat from"

in the day of your eating from it, dying you shall die
of your eating → אֲכָלְךָ (akhalka) — infinitive construct of אכל (akhal, "to eat, consume") with second person masculine singular suffix. Temporal, not conditional: "when you eat."
dying → מוֹת (mot) — infinitive absolute of מות (mut, "to die"). The intensifying construction — opens dying as a state entered, processual, not a single event.
you shall die → תָּמוּת (tamut) — second person masculine singular imperfect of מות. The finite verb completing the construction.
The infinitive absolute (mot) initiates a condition. The finite verb (tamut) completes it. Between them is the entire span of mortal life.

Cain "Given by God" (to acquire)
The firstborn Murdered Abel Envious / deceptive
Represents maybe something like our potential to mimic God
Taking life and death into our own hands Like Cain did. Forgetting our place.

Abel (A whif of air / Transient) The after-thought
Favored by God Murdered by Cain
Represents something like our potential to live in perfect harmony with God

Seth (Put in place of / Plant firm)
The replacement for Abel, since we ruled Abel out by killing him
Lived 912 years

The tree of knowing good and evil
The → הַ (ha) — definite article. Specific, singular, set apart.
tree → עֵץ (ets) — tree, wood, timber.
Possibly rooted in עצה/עצם suggesting firmness, substance, structure. The thing that holds and transmits.
of the knowing → דַּעַת (da'at) — verbal noun from ידע (yada). Not information but transformative participatory contact. The verb for sexual union, covenant intimacy, craft mastery. You don't yada something and remain what you were.
of good → טוֹב (tov) — functional, ripe, beautiful, beneficial, fit-for-purpose, complete.
and → וָ (va) — conjunction, but in paired constructions can signal spectrum, tension, or totality. The hinge between poles.
evil → רָע (ra) — harmful, broken, spoiled, ugly, calamitous, dysfunctional. Not abstract metaphysical evil but the experienced quality of things that damage and diminish. Bad fruit, bad road, bad outcome.

Enoch (To initiate / dedicate)
Civilization arises from the Cain impulse
"And he became a builder of a city, and he called the name of the city after the name of his son, Enoch." Genesis 4:17

Irad
(Irad) — most likely from עִיר (ir), "city" or "watcher/guardian."
The עִירִין (irin) are the Watchers in Daniel 4 and the Enochic literature. But in the civic reading:
Enoch founds the city (Gen 4:17 — Cain names a city after him), and Irad is what the city produces

Mehuja'el (מְחוּיָאֵל) Possible decompositions:
"Struck by Yah-God" — מְחוּ + יָ + אֵל — two divine names compounded with violence
"Blotted out by God" — מחה + אֵל — erasure, not impact (same root as God erasing humanity in Gen 6:7)
"God reveals/declares" — חוה + אֵל — same root as Chavvah/Eve
"God gives life" — חיה + אֵל — root of mechayeh hametim, "reviver of the dead"
The tension: the name is genuinely undecidable between erasure, revelation, and life-giving.
In the Cainite civilizational sequence, this is the generation where divine authority gets captured by human power and the ambiguity is the meaning.
The sacred now means whatever the structure needs it to mean.

Methushael (מְתוּשָׁאֵל) Possible decompositions:
"Man of God" — מְתוּ (man, cognate with Akkadian mutu) + אֵל " Man of Sheol" — מְתוּ + שְׁאוֹל, the divine and the grave compressed into one name
"Death of God" — מוּת (death) + אֵל, same consonants rearranged
"Demanded of God" — שָׁאַל (to ask/demand) + אֵל, compare Samuel/Shemu'el

Lamech (לֶמֶךְ) Possible decompositions:
"Brought low" — from מוך (mukh), to be poor or diminished, with a ל- prefix
"Powerful one" — cognate with Arabic lamaka, to be strong
"To what purpose?" — from למה (lamah), "why?", the questioning/despairing one
"Priestly servant" — possibly from Sumerian/Akkadian lumakku, a cultic office

Enosh (אֱנוֹשׁ) Possible decompositions:
"Mortal man" — enosh as frail, perishable man, distinct from adam (earthling) and ish (individual)
"Sick/incurable" — from אנש, to be weak, desperate, terminal (Jeremiah 17:9, the heart "desperately wicked")
"Companionable" — minority etymology, cognate with Arabic ins (humankind) and uns (intimacy)
"Seth also had a son, and he named him Enosh. At that time people began to call on the name of the Lord." Genesis 4:26

Kenan "Nester" (nest)
Someone who builds a nest
Lived 910 years

Enoch (To initiate / dedicate)
Lived 365 years
Enoch walked with God he was no more, for God took him

Methuselah (his death sends a projectile)
When this one dies, something is launched.
Lived 969 years
He dies the year of the flood

Lamech see etymology above
He names his son Noah and says: "This one will comfort us from our work and the toil of our hands, from the ground which the LORD has cursed."
Lived 777 years
He dies 5 years before the flood

Noah "Rest" or "Settle"
Builds the Ark and survives the cataclysm
Bridge between the old world and the new
The people who survived the Cataclysm split into three cultures
The Bible tells the story from a Shem perspective So Japheth and Ham are secondary
Lived 950 years
This is the first splitting / division When one becomes many
The symbolism of Jesus (God's son) "dying on the cross" suddenly seems apt.
"The Crux of the matter" God is the Word He sends his son, the Word in flesh
It is perfect, but upon contact with humanity it is killed on the cross. The cross is a universal symbol for division.

Shem "Name" (Name / essence / substance
Covers up father's nakedness
"Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem." Genesis 9:26
They chose to cover up but preserve old world knowledge. The Abrahamic line.
Shem also intermixed culture and most likely religion with Japheth and Ham on various occasions
Lived 600 years

Ham "The Hot One"
saw his father's nakedness, but his brothers refused to look.
This culture decided to openly expose and take advantage of old world knowledge.
They build powerful empires that they can't sustain long term
For insight into Ham cultures, look at the earliest known powerful empires, like ancient Egypt, Mesopotamia, and many others.
Ham culture intermixed with Shem and Japheth on many occasions along the march of time

Japheth "The Expanded One" "The One Who Spreads Wide"
Covers up father's nakedness
"May God enlarge Japheth, and let him dwell in the tents of Shem. and let Canaan be his servant."
It seems clear that Shem and Japheth part on good terms, "Let them dwell in our tents". The Canaan-servant comment seems important:
They understand that Ham will be strong initially, but that they can't sustain it. So it is Canaan (and his sons), not Ham that will one day have to serve Japheth.
This culture refuses to exploit old world knowledge, and branches off into various cultures with their own mythologies
For more insight into Japheth cultures, look into the older world religions that did not branch out of Shem.
The Hindu religions, Greek/Norse mythology, and many others. Likely intermixed with Shem and Ham cultures on many occasions

Abram "Exalted father"
Becomes Abraham the added "ha" represents a piece of God added to his name / essence after their covenant was made
Lives 175 years

Hagar "Foreigner" uncertain etymology
Sarah's egyptian handmaid
Sarah exiles her, and God hears her crying in the wilderness

Keturah "Incense" or "perfumed one"
After Sarah dies, Abraham fathers several sons with Keturah

Isaac "He laughs"
Abram and Sarah laughs when God tells them to have another child, due to their old age. God tells them to name him
Yitzhak — "he laughs."
"God has made laughter for me. Everyone who hears will laugh with me."

Midian
Where Moses flees and meets his wife

Rebekah
Isaac's wife and mother of his sons
"Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples from within you shall be divided; the one shall be stronger than the other, the elder shall serve the younger." (Genesis 25:23)

Jacob / Israel Name: יַעֲקֹב (Ya'aqov) — from עקב ('aqev), "heel."
Named because he was born grasping his twin Esau's heel (Genesis 25:26).
The root also carries "to follow at the heel," "to supplant," "to deceive."
Renamed: יִשְׂרָאֵל (Yisra'el) — given after wrestling the unnamed figure at the Jabbok ford (Genesis 32:28).
The text glosses it as "you have striven (sarita) with God and with men, and have prevailed."
Often translated "He Who Wrestles With God"
Walks away with a permanent limp.

Moses "The born one"
Raised as Egyptian royalty.
After he kills an Egyptian taskmaster for beating a Hebrew slave he flees to Midian, and meets his wife
called by God to liberate the Hebrews
He acts as a mediator between God and humanity.
He attains his connection with God while working near mount Sinai
He remains relatively poor materialistically his whole life, and is not allowed to enter the promised land, because he disrespected the Lord in front of his people

Aaron The High Priest
After God consumes his first and second son, Moses tells him
"This is what the LORD meant when He said, 'through those near to me I will be sanctified, and before all the people I will be glorified.'"
Aaron's response was silence

Zipporah "Bird"
daughter of Jethro the Midianite priest

Gershom "Stranger there"
"I have been a stranger (ger) in a foreign land."

Nadab "generous" or "willing"
Sees God and lives But then disrespects God somehow and is
consumed by God

Abihu "He is my father"
Same story as Nadab Also consumed by God

Eleazar "God has helped"
Becomes high priest after Aaron dies

Ithamar Possibly "island/land of palms" Etymology uncertain
Oversees the Gershonites and Merarites in the tabernacle construction.

Onan "Vigor, strength"
Spills his seed on the ground instead of in Tamar
God destroys him

Er
Destroyed by God For being wicked

Shelah
Promised to Tamar, but Judah withholds

Tamar
Judah's daughter-in-law She seduces Judah and gets twins
She becomes the mother of the entire royal path to Jesus Christ

Hezron "Enclosed" or "of the settlement"
Has a wife Abijah "My Father is Jah" "God is my Father"
This appears to symbolize

Onam "my potency/vigor"
Same root as Ben-Oni this translation is reductive

Ozem
possibly from עצם ( atsam) "to be strong" or "to be numerous"

Nahshon
Nahshon (נַחְשׁוֹן) — from נָחָשׁ (nachash, "serpent") with an intensive suffix. "The great serpent" or "the diviner."
This is a loaded name nachash is the Eden serpent word, but also carries connotations of bronze/copper (נְחֹשֶׁת, nechoshet) and divination/observation.
Nahshon is prince of Judah in the wilderness (Numbers 2:3)

Boaz
Possibly בּוֹ (bo, "in him") + עַז (az, "strength"): "in him is strength."

Jesse
Yishai — uncertain and disputed. Possibly from יֵשׁ (yesh, "there is/exists") + a divine suffix: "God exists" or "man of God."

David "Beloved"
From דוד (dod, "beloved, uncle, friend"). The word also carries erotic connotations Song of Songs uses דוֹדִי (dodi, "my beloved") throughout. The king is the beloved one.

Azubah "Forsaken"
I think the testament is making a point about quality over quantity
First wife

Ephrath "Fruitful"
Note that she only has one son yet she is the one called Fruitful
And her legacy explains why
Second wife after Azubah's death

Hur "Free/noble" or "White/pale"
He was close and trusted by Moses and Aaron
Born a slave, then freed in Exodus by God

Shobal "flowing/extending/path"
Flowing, covering, harvesting. Or: the path that extends, the garment that covers, the season that gathers

Bezalel "In the shadow of God" "In God's Image"
A slave's great-grandson, filled with divine spirit, building the house of God in the wilderness.

Kiriath-Jaerim "City of Forests"
the city where the Ark of the Covenant rested for twenty years after the Philistines returned it (1 Samuel 7:1-2)
Beth-Gader "House of the Wall" "House of the enclosure"

Ben-Oni Benjamin "son of the right hand"
Ben-Oni (בֶּן־אוֹנִי)
Rachel's name for her second son, spoken as she dies in childbirth (Genesis 35:18).
The root און (on) carries simultaneous meanings that cannot be separated without losing the name's force: sexual/procreative potency, vital strength, sorrow, and trouble.
Rachel's naming collapses all of these into a single utterance — this child is the son of her husband's virility, the son of her own life-force spent, and the son of her death-agony, all at once. The act that created him is the act that destroyed her.
Jacob immediately renames him Benjamin ("son of the right hand"), replacing the raw embodied truth with something politically auspicious

Nimrod "Rebel"
"Mighty hunter before the Lord"
He is the founder of Babylon and other powerful civilizations
"And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Akkad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar. From that land he went out to Assyria, and built Nineveh, and Rehoboth-Ir, and Calah, and Resen between Nineveh and Calah — that is the great city."

Gomer
traditionally identified with the Cimmerians (Akkadian Gimirrāya), an Indo-European people who appeared north of the Black Sea and later swept into Anatolia

Magog "the far north"
Gog from Magog
Madai Iranian people of the Zagros, capital Ecbatana, later absorbed into the Achaemenid empire
Javan the Ionians (Greek Iáwones, Hebrew Yāwān)

Tubal & Meshech
The Tabal and Mushki of Assyrian records, Anatolian kingdoms in eastern/central Asia Minor, post-Hittite. Herodotus' Tibarenoi and Moschoi.

Tiras
most likely the Tyrsenoi/Tyrrhenians, i.e., the sea peoples that Greek tradition links to the Etruscans' origin.

Ashkenaz
Scythians (Pontic steppe)
Jeremiah 51:27 calls up "the kingdoms of Ararat, Minni, and Ashkenaz" against Babylon
Later appropriated, this is not the same as the Ashkenazi Jews

Togarmah
Tegarama in Hittite records, Til-garimmu in Assyrian, a city and region in eastern Anatolia (probably near modern Gürün in Turkey, in the upper Euphrates area).
It was a significant Late Bronze Age center, destroyed by Sennacherib in 695 BCE.
Ezekiel 27:14 says "Beth-togarmah exchanged horses, war horses, and mules for your wares" —
Eastern Anatolia was indeed prime horse-breeding country, and this fits the Cappadocian/Armenian highland economy precisely.
Elishah almost certainly Alashiya, the Bronze Age name for Cyprus

Tarshish
the famously contested one. Candidates:
Tartessos in southwestern Spain (Guadalquivir region) — a wealthy Phoenician trading partner known for silver, tin, lead.
This is the dominant scholarly identification and fits Ezekiel 27:12 ("Tarshish did business with you because of your great wealth of every kind; silver, iron, tin, and lead they exchanged for your wares").
Tarsus in Cilicia (southern Anatolia) — phonetically tempting, geographically much closer.
Sardinia — there's a 9th c. BCE Phoenician inscription from Nora, Sardinia, that contains the word tršš, which some read as Tarshish.
A generic "far west" — by the time of Jonah, "ships of Tarshish" had become an idiom for large oceangoing vessels, and "fleeing to Tarshish" meant fleeing to the end of the known world (which is the whole point of Jonah going there to escape Nineveh — opposite direction, edge of the map).

Kittim
Kition, the major Phoenician city-state on Cyprus (modern Larnaca). Originally just "people of Kition," then broadened to mean Cypriots generally, then broadened again to mean "westerners across the sea."
By the Hellenistic period it covers Macedonians and Greeks (1 Maccabees calls Alexander a king "from the land of Kittim"),
and by the Roman period the Qumran sectarians use Kittim as their code-word for the Romans — the War Scroll's eschatological enemy is "the Kittim of Asshur" and "the Kittim in Egypt." So Kittim has this fascinating semantic drift: Cypriot city → Cyprus → Greeks → Romans → eschatological western oppressor.
It tracks the Levantine experience of "who is currently invading us from the sea."

Adah (עָדָה) Possible decompositions:
"Ornament/adornment" — from עדה ( adah), to adorn, to deck oneself
"Witness/testimony" — from עֵד ( ed), witness, with a feminine suffix
"To advance/pass by" — from עדה (adah), to pass, to move forward
"Pleasure/delight" — from עדן (eden), same consonantal field as Eden itself

Zillah (צִלָּה) Possible decompositions:
"Shadow" — from צֵל (tzel), shadow, shade
"Ringing/clanging" — from צלל (tzalal), to ring, to vibrate, to resound
"Protection" — צֵל as shelter, "in the shadow of," as in Psalm 91:1
"Dark one" — shadow as absence of light, the hidden
Naamah (Pleasant / To be agreeable / She who is pleasant) F
